landingFaqs.question: My vision seems normal. Do I still need a retina check?
landingFaqs.answer: Often, yes. Conditions like diabetic retinopathy and macular degeneration can develop before vision is noticeably affected. Regular screening catches changes while they're most manageable.

landingFaqs.question: How often should people with diabetes have their retina examined?
landingFaqs.answer: Generally once a year, even without symptoms. If changes are already present, more frequent monitoring may be recommended based on what we find.

landingFaqs.question: What happens if retinal changes are found early?
landingFaqs.answer: Many early-stage changes simply require closer monitoring, not immediate treatment. Catching things early gives us more options and more time to protect your vision.

educationalInsight.title: Why Retinal Screening Matters Even If Your Vision Seems Normal
educationalInsight.body: Many retinal conditions develop gradually and may not affect vision until they become more advanced. Conditions such as diabetic retinopathy and age-related macular degeneration often benefit from early detection and regular monitoring. The purpose of retinal evaluation is not simply to diagnose disease -- it's to identify changes early enough that vision can be protected whenever possible.
prioritizeIf: You have Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes, any duration
It has been more than a year since your last dilated exam
You are over 50 with no recent retina check
You have a family history of macular degeneration

doctorsPerspective: One of the challenges with retinal disease is that patients often expect symptoms to warn them when something is wrong. Unfortunately, that isn't always the case. Many retinal conditions develop quietly and may not affect vision until later stages. Regular retinal examinations allow us to detect changes early, monitor them over time, and decide whether treatment is needed. Often, the most important thing we provide is clarity about whether your retina is healthy today and what should happen next.
